refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across 1000+ control cycles under worst-case network conditions within the agreed envelope.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing delay
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics
FR-TEL-006: Implement safety interlocks to prevent unsafe operation if latency exceeds threshold

candidate_dps: DP-A: Distributed real-time architecture with edge processing on vehicle, optimized network codec, and operator-side command buffering to minimize round-trip latency
DP-B: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with local network gateway caching to reduce perceived latency
DP-C: Hybrid edge-cloud with predictive command interpolation and adaptive bitrate streaming to maintain latency under variable network conditions
DP-D: Direct peer-to-peer communication with local network optimization and hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Distributed real-time architecture with edge processing on vehicle, optimized network codec, and operator-side command buffering to minimize round-trip latency

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition frequency (FR-TEL-001) and network bandwidth (FR-TEL-002) is managed via adaptive codec selection
Coupling between command transmission (FR-TEL-003) and actuator execution (FR-TEL-004) is decoupled via command queue with timestamp validation
Safety interlock (FR-TEL-006) is independent of latency optimization logic, reducing coupling risk
coupling_check.notes: DP-A avoids cloud round-trip latency penalty and provides deterministic edge processing. Codec optimization and command buffering are orthogonal concerns, enabling independent tuning. Safety interlocks operate at vehicle level, decoupled from network optimization.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient compute capacity for real-time edge processing (sensor fusion, codec). Risk: Legacy vehicle platforms may lack required CPU/GPU. Mitigation: Validate compute budget in vehicle architecture review.

Assumption: Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Risk: Actual field conditions may exceed assumptions. Mitigation: Implement latency monitoring and graceful degradation (FR-TEL-005, FR-TEL-006).

Assumption: Operator-side command buffering does not introduce unacceptable control lag. Risk: Buffering may mask network delays, degrading operator perception. Mitigation: Transparent latency reporting to operator; operator training on latency awareness.

Assumption: Optimized codec maintains acceptable video/sensor fidelity at target bitrate. Risk: Aggressive compression may degrade situational awareness. Mitigation: Operator acceptance testing with representative video streams.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) target bitrate and codec selection strategy (H.264, H.265, or custom), (2) edge processing pipeline (sensor fusion, frame capture, encoding), (3) command buffering strategy (FIFO, priority queue, timestamp validation), (4) latency measurement points (sensor → network, network → actuator), (5) graceful degradation thresholds (when to reduce frame rate, resolution, or alert operator).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: real-time sensor acquisition and encoding on vehicle, network transmission/reception, operator command decoding and execution, latency monitoring, and safety interlock enforcement. Owns the end-to-end latency budget and is responsible for meeting the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor telemetry (video, position, orientation, proximity, link health metrics) to operator console and safety services. Includes latency metadata for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) from remote operator console via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (e.g., latency threshold exceeded, network link degraded). Outputs safety-critical events (e.g., latency violation, command timeout) to Safety Services for interlock enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with network infrastructure for bidirectional communication. Owns codec selection, packet prioritization, and link health monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own teleoperation latency responsibility. Teleoperation is a distinct subsystem with specialized real-time requirements. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership and obscure latency accountability.

Vehicle owns edge processing (sensor acquisition, encoding) but does not own operator-side command buffering, network codec selection, or operator console logic. Latency is end-to-end; assigning to Vehicle alone would split responsibility and create coupling across system boundaries.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of all teleoperation-specific logic: sensor encoding, network transmission, command decoding, latency monitoring, and safety interlocks. Latency budget is end-to-end; Teleoperation module spans vehicle and operator sides, enabling unified ownership and accountability. High containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement (e.g., latency threshold interlocks) but does not own the teleoperation communication logic itself. Assigning latency optimization to Safety Services would conflate safety policy with real-time optimization, reducing clarity and increasing coupling.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008 because: (1) it encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic (sensor encoding, network transmission, command decoding, latency monitoring), (2) it spans both vehicle and operator sides, enabling end-to-end latency accountability, (3) it owns the network codec and buffering strategies that directly impact latency, (4) it interfaces with Safety Services for interlock enforcement but retains optimization responsibility, (5) it minimizes diffusion of latency-critical logic across multiple modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a monolith if all real-time optimization logic is co-located. Mitigation: Decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (Vehicle Teleoperation Agent, Operator Console, Network Codec, Latency Monitor) at the next decomposition level.

Risk: Teleoperation module may have tight coupling with Vehicle hardware (sensor interfaces, actuator control). Mitigation: Define clear hardware abstraction layer (HAL) interfaces to decouple Teleoperation logic from vehicle-specific implementations.

Risk: Teleoperation module may conflict with Safety Services on interlock enforcement (e.g., who decides to disable teleoperation if latency exceeds threshold?). Mitigation: Clarify ownership: Teleoperation owns latency monitoring and reporting; Safety Services owns policy enforcement and interlock actuation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module boundary to explicitly exclude: (1) vehicle-specific sensor drivers (owned by Vehicle), (2) operator console UI/UX (owned by operator-side system), (3) safety policy logic (owned by Safety Services). Clarify that Teleoperation owns the communication pipeline, latency measurement, and graceful degradation logic, but delegates safety interlock actuation to Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP-A (distributed real-time architecture with edge processing, optimized codec, and command buffering) directly addresses the refined FR (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). DP-A avoids cloud round-trip latency, enables deterministic edge processing, and provides mechanisms for latency monitoring and graceful degradation. No uncoupled FRs or missing DPs identified. Coupling between sub-FRs is managed via orthogonal design choices (codec selection, command queue, safety interlocks).

diffusion_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may diffuse latency-critical logic across vehicle and operator sides if interfaces are not well-defined. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with latency budgets for each segment.

Risk: Network Link interface may become a bottleneck if codec selection and packet prioritization are not owned by Teleoperation. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns codec and prioritization logic; Network Link is a passive transport interface.

Risk: Safety Services may introduce latency if interlock enforcement is synchronous. Mitigation: Teleoperation reports latency metrics asynchronously; Safety Services enforces interlocks independently.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) of latency-critical logic. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. Ownership is clear: Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency accountability; Safety Services owns policy enforcement; Vehicle owns edge processing hardware.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-008 is feasible via: (1) latency measurement instrumentation in Teleoperation module (timestamp at sensor, network, actuator), (2) network simulation testbed with agreed conditions, (3) operator acceptance testing with representative tasks, (4) field validation under real network conditions. Teleoperation module boundary enables end-to-end measurement without cross-module dependencies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (sensor acquisition frequency), operator console (command buffering), and Safety Services (interlock enforcement) are defined via external interfaces. Verification points are traceable to module boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify codec selection strategy, edge processing pipeline, command buffering algorithm, latency measurement points, and graceful degradation thresholds.
Decompose Teleoperation module into sub-modules (Vehicle Teleoperation Agent, Operator Console, Network Codec, Latency Monitor) at the next level to reduce monolithic risk.
Define latency budgets for each interface segment (sensor → encoding, encoding → network, network → decoding, decoding → actuator) to enable hierarchical verification.
Establish interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link with explicit latency and jitter requirements.
Clarify ownership boundary between Teleoperation (latency optimization) and Safety Services (interlock enforcement) to avoid coupling and confusion.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to enable detailed design and verification. The refined FR identifies six sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, telemetry transmission, command reception, actuator execution, link health monitoring, safety interlocks) that must be individually designed and verified. DP-A provides a coherent solution strategy, and Teleoperation module provides a clear ownership boundary. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate latency budgets, define interface contracts, and detail implementation strategies for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, proximity) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with minimal latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing delay
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report communication link health and latency metrics
FR-TEL-006: Implement safety interlocks to prevent unsafe operation if latency exceeds threshold

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient compute capacity (CPU/GPU) for real-time edge processing (sensor fusion, codec).
Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Operator-side command buffering does not introduce unacceptable control lag or mask network delays.
Optimized codec maintains acceptable video/sensor fidelity at target bitrate.
Safety Services can enforce interlocks asynchronously without introducing latency.
Teleoperation module can measure end-to-end latency with sufficient precision (millisecond granularity).
open_risks: Risk: Legacy vehicle platforms may lack required compute capacity for real-time edge processing. Mitigation: Validate compute budget in vehicle architecture review.
Risk: Actual field network conditions may exceed agreed assumptions. Mitigation: Implement latency monitoring and graceful degradation; operator training on latency awareness.
Risk: Aggressive codec compression may degrade situational awareness. Mitigation: Operator acceptance testing with representative video streams.
Risk: Teleoperation module may become a monolith if all real-time logic is co-located. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-modules at next level.
Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Vehicle hardware may reduce portability. Mitigation: Define clear hardware abstraction layer (HAL) interfaces.
Risk: Ownership boundary between Teleoperation and Safety Services may be ambiguous. Mitigation: Clarify that Teleoperation owns latency monitoring; Safety Services owns policy enforcement.
evidence_needed: Vehicle compute capacity specification (CPU, GPU, memory, real-time OS support).
Agreed network conditions specification (bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Codec performance data (bitrate vs. fidelity, encoding/decoding latency).
Operator acceptance test results (latency perception, control responsiveness, situational awareness).
Field validation data (actual network conditions, latency distribution, p95 achievement).
Safety interlock specification (latency threshold, enforcement mechanism, operator notification).
Latency measurement instrumentation design (timestamp sources, precision, accuracy).
